domains: Substrate service pattern: @SubstrateService + SubstrateServiceContract + ServiceCategory + service-universal versioned contracts + common-imprint typed ledgers + common-process spawning
Digikoma factory pattern: 5-target Package.swift (Tool + Service + FoundationModelsTool + cli + tests) per package, mirroring digikoma-hello-world

Schema versioning across three independent axes (set version pinned in schemaSetRefs[0].set, family version pinned in file-level schemaVersion, record discriminator per typed Codable via SemanticVersionable)
Per-resource queue management primitive (pending/claimed/applied/failed atomic-rename state machine in ResourceQueue<Intent>)
Agent-side typed emitter (CLI + library composition; CLI requires explicit --harness + --session-id to prevent cross-session misrouting)

futureOperatorGuidance: When adding a new digikoma: copy digikoma-hello-world's 5-target Package.swift layout (Tool + Service + FoundationModelsTool + cli + tests) + the canonical ImprintLedger pattern. Never start from scratch.
When adding a new service contract: copy cli-rebuild-service-contracts v0.1.0's layout + savepoint-service-contracts v0.1.0 as a second reference. service-universal is the canonical home.

When adding a new event kind: extend SubstrateEvent + SubstrateEventKind + (if needed) ServiceCategory in common-service. Keep payload primitive (file path, IDs) so common-service stays free of schema-universal deps.

Before authoring ANY primitive in common-service: enumerate ls common-* + grep public structures across all common-* package sources. If the concept exists, use it. SubprocessSpawner + ServiceLedger both died pre-ship from skipping this check the first time.
savepoint-cli emit MUST require --harness + --session-id; env-var inference is forbidden because parallel sessions across harnesses (claude-code + codex + future) make it unreliable.
Before committing: git diff --cached --name-only verifies scope matches intent. Especially important for multi-repo commits (submodule + mono pin) — each index needs separate check.

When fs-touch-events stream produces real events (Wave 6), digikoma-savepoint v0.2 fills CommitArtifactModel.fsEventRefs[] by reading the ledger directly (no IPC). Plan for that consumer at v0.2 design time.
When the substrate event-bus daemon eventually registers services in-process, DigikomaSavepointService.condition bumps from .never to .always to replace savepointd's spawn dispatch.
sharpEdges: Bash cwd persists between invocations — if a prior `cd <relative>` failed, subsequent relative paths point at the wrong place; always cd absolute first
scoped git add does NOT protect against pre-staged changes from prior operations or other sessions — diff --cached EVERY time
claude-code project slug is workspace-root-derived, not cwd-derived; scanning ~/.claude/projects/*/ is robust against the slug-derivation rule changing
CommonProcessExecutionKit (not CommonProcess) exports CommandSpec.run() — link errors otherwise
@available(macOS 26+) gated targets need fallback runtime paths or pre-26 callsites break — keep a v0.1 placeholder body in FoundationModelsTool targets
agents/<slug>/ is often itself a git submodule — bumping pins requires per-submodule commit + mono superproject commit chain
shinji-techo v0.2.0 requires an operator-authored quote per record; backfill placeholder is detectable as 'no operator-authored quote' and is a code smell

m.x: Push lane added to the substrate's queue+spawn+receipt pattern. Reusable lessons: server-side dispatch beats client-side iteration; intents must live outside working trees; existing libraries get reused not rewritten when operator names them.

highlights: DOCTRINE — request-side stays thin. CLIs (savepoint-cli) construct + dispatch typed intents to daemons (savepointd); they do NOT iterate over targets or coordinate ordering. Submodule walking, fanout, children-first ordering, retry policy, parallelism — all dispatch-layer concerns owned by the service that receives the intent. The operator named this explicitly: 'savepoint-cli should request for uploads from savepointd - not perform it itself'.

DOCTRINE — intents live outside the working trees they reference. CommitRequestModel + PushIntentModel intent files staged inside the target repo will trip git-status cleanness checks (?? untracked → dirty → rejection). Canonical staging is ~/.local/share/savepoint/{push-staging,staging}/ for client-side construction, and ~/.local/share/savepointd/queues/<git-dir-hash>/pending/ for queued dispatch. Tests must mirror — write intents to a tmp dir distinct from the work tree.

DOCTRINE — reuse existing libraries instead of replicating logic. Before authoring git plumbing in DigikomaGitPushTool, operator surfaced SwiftUniversalGitCore from swift-git-cli (paths visible at swift-universal/private/universal/domain/tooling/spm/swift-git-cli/Sources/SwiftUniversalGitCore/). It already had runGit, GitResult, GitAutomationPolicy.isExternalOrg, parseSubmoduleEntries, requireGitRepo, gitChangedFileCount. Substrate has no central registry of utility libraries — practice: grep for existing functionality before writing primitives.

PATTERN — factory parity packages ship 5 targets: Tool (the actual work) + Service (subscribes to SubstrateEvent kind) + FoundationModelsTool (on-device LLM glue, FoundationModels-gated with @available(macOS 26+)) + cli (one-shot subprocess entry) + Imprint (typed SubstrateLedgerEntry → per-digikoma execution receipts at ~/.local/share/<slug>/ledgers/). digikoma-git-push, digikoma-savepoint, digikoma-hello-world all share this shape now. Three concrete proof cases lock the factory pattern in.

PATTERN — two parallel ledgers per substrate operation. Per-service receipt (e.g. PushImprint in savepointd: queue routing + worker exit code, asks 'did dispatch succeed?') + per-digikoma receipt (e.g. DigikomaGitPushImprint: actual outcome of the work, asks 'did the action succeed and what changed?'). Audit can drill from either side. Both use SubstrateLedgerWriter<Entry: SubstrateLedgerEntry> from common-imprint.

PATTERN — server-side fanout for recursive operations. PushService.handleRecursive walks submodules via 'git submodule foreach --recursive --quiet echo $displaypath', sorts deepest-first for children-before-parent ordering, processes each through a per-.git queue, then drives the parent push only if all children succeeded. Single client request → N typed child intents synthesized server-side. Parent push gets recurseSubmodules=false to prevent infinite recursion.

COMMAND — savepoint-cli push: thin client interface for substrate-managed git push. Required: --harness <name> + --session-id <id>. Optional: --recurse-submodules (server fans out), --dry-run (intent + report no push), --force-with-lease (non-FF allowed), --remote/--branch (override @{u} resolution). Output is savepointd's TriggerImprint JSON on stdout.

ENVIRONMENT — three binaries symlinked at ~/.local/bin/: savepointd (queue manager, hosts SavepointService + PushService side-by-side), savepoint-cli (agent-side thin client with emit + push subcommands), digikoma-git-push (per-execution push worker spawned by savepointd, also runnable standalone via --intent <path>). Each release-built via swift build -c release + ln -sf into ~/.local/bin/.
p.summary: Push lane added to the substrate's queue+spawn+receipt pattern. Reusable lessons: server-side dispatch beats client-side iteration; intents must live outside working trees; existing libraries get reused not rewritten when operator names them.

m.x: Substrate is multi-actor in observable practice, not just in doctrine. The push-factory cleanness rail catches blockers correctly; autonomous sync agents resolve them in parallel. mono root's 34 stale unmerged paths is operator-territory cleanup beyond autonomous resolution.

highlights: PATTERN — push-cleanness rejection is informational, not just protective. When DigikomaGitPushTool.ensureClean() rejects with dirtyWorkingTree, the stderr it captures (full `git status --porcelain=v1` output) is also the per-digikoma ledger's stderr payload. Operator can read the ledger to know exactly what's blocking + which other actor (parallel session, autonomous sync agent, operator) owns each file. The receipt is the diagnosis.

PATTERN — autonomous-sync agents observed live. The substrate has background actors (savepointd + push companions) that batch-commit uncommitted state via 'chore: sync local changes' messages + push autonomously. These run on their own cadence, independent of any agent session. Heuristic: if a submodule is dirty when you check + clean when you check 30 min later, that's a sync-agent footprint. Look for 'chore: sync ...' or 'chore(root): refresh ...' commits to confirm.

PATTERN — substrate as multi-actor working tree. An agent session does NOT have exclusive ownership of `git status` output. Between any two `git status` calls, the substrate may have: (a) committed other-session work autonomously, (b) pushed it upstream, (c) refreshed submodule pointers, (d) added new entries to /private/.wrkstrm/* runtime paths. Defensive practice: re-check status before each commit; assume any 'untracked' file might belong to another actor; never `git add -A`.

DOCTRINE — operator territory vs autonomous territory. Bulk-resolving 34 unmerged paths spanning .gitmodules + ~10 submodule pointers + codex auth/sqlite runtime state is OPERATOR TERRITORY. Risk profile: wrong submodule pointer choice loses another session's work; wrong .gitmodules choice corrupts the structure; wrong codex auth choice breaks working authentication. AskUserQuestion before bulk-resolving any merge state >5 files spanning multiple risk surfaces. The substrate's autonomous agents do submodule-pointer sync on a single repo — they don't bulk-resolve cross-submodule merge conflicts. That mode requires a human reading the diff.

COMMAND — `git diff HEAD origin/main -- <path1> <path2>` is the right introspection before merge. Shows whether remote already has newer SHAs at those paths (in which case merge will favor remote with no conflict + my work is preserved if it's already upstream via other route), or whether remote diverged from local (true conflict requiring resolution). This is how I confirmed origin/main already had newer pointers than my local for the 4 collectives — meaning the autonomous sync had already swept my work upstream + the merge would just fast-forward.

COMMAND — `git ls-files --unmerged | wc -l` divided by 3 gives a quick count of unmerged paths (each path has up to 3 stage entries: base, ours, theirs). Combined with `git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U`, gives both count + file list. Useful for surfacing the scale of a merge state before deciding how to handle it.

ENVIRONMENT — mono root has its own behind-state cadence driven by autonomous sync agents writing 'chore(mono): sync ...' commits on origin. Local catches up via `git pull --ff-only` or `git merge --no-edit origin/main`; bulk push of substantial local-only commit ranges (1000+ ahead) requires reconciling the cadence first. Don't assume mono root is push-eligible just because submodules are.
p.summary: Substrate is multi-actor in observable practice, not just in doctrine. The push-factory cleanness rail catches blockers correctly; autonomous sync agents resolve them in parallel. mono root's 34 stale unmerged paths is operator-territory cleanup beyond autonomous resolution.

qt.t: the working tree is NOT single-actor

m.x: Substrate teaches itself through types, not through doc-discovery. Folder shape carries discipline. Audit-before-author saves duplication every time. Chemistry layer-cake (elements/molecules/compounds/mixtures) is the substrate's natural type-theory layout — all four layers now have typed contracts.

highlights: DOCTRINE — substrate teaches itself through TYPES, not through DOC-DISCOVERY. When typed contracts live only in DocC articles, agents accidentally miss them. When typed contracts are REQUIRED at every surface an agent touches (every CLI invocation, every bundle manifest, every typed record's class-name discriminator), agents can't miss them. Type-system enforcement of doctrine is the substrate's natural pattern. Examples shipped this session: IncidentBehaviorContract REQUIRED on every IncidentModel (can't open an incident without naming who pauses), workGraphNode REQUIRED on every CLIABundleManifestModel v0.2.0 (can't author a bundle without declaring its work-graph node identity), KickoffMeetingReceiptModel.isValidKickoffReceipt enforces non-empty cujs+designMockups+signOff at validation boundaries (launch-review gates against this).

DOCTRINE — chemistry layer-cake is the substrate's natural type-theory layout. Four layers now fully typed: ELEMENTS (typed schema records like BeadsIssue, KickoffMeetingReceiptModel, WorkflowGraphNode), MOLECULES (typed workflows like BeadsMolecule, WorkflowGraphDoctrine, KeikoWorkflow), COMPOUNDS (typed assemblies like SubstrateCompoundModel; apps/products/releases/vaults/services/factories), MIXTURES (the substrate workspace itself, implicit). Plus BONDS (LinkRefModel substrate-wide + BeadsBondPoint bead-scoped), REACTIONS (typed via WorkflowRunDiscipline invariants + WorkGraphEventModel emissions), CATALYSTS (.clia bundles driving reactions). The substrate's typed primitives self-host their own description; SubstrateCompoundModel describes apps including the kanban-app that describes substrate compounds.

DOCTRINE — folder shape carries discipline. schema-universal's layout (domain/<bucket>/schema-families/<family>/v<X.Y.Z>/spm/<family>-v<XXX-YYY-ZZZ>/) FORCES every new typed contract to declare its domain bucket + version + SPM package shape up front. That's why this session's 7 family ships landed cleanly — placement decisions were OBVIOUS because the folder shape pre-grouped semantically-adjacent families. workflow-graph-doctrine-schemas → system/ (sibling to workflow-schemas / workstream-schemas / thread-schemas); incident-schemas → system/ (sibling to thread-schemas, peer to other coordinator types); clia-bundle-schemas → ai/ (sibling to foundation-adapter-training-schemas + clia-session-schemas); org-mission-statement-schemas → org/ (sibling to org-company-schemas). When I'd been tempted to invent a new folder shape (a one-off file outside the convention), the folder structure pushed back: 'find your siblings first.'

DOCTRINE — audit-before-author saves duplication EVERY time. Task #57 discovered that ~90% of the work-graph protocol I was about to author from scratch already existed in workflow-schemas v0.1.0 (24-field WorkflowModel + Lifecycle + Runtime + Keiko PRD/CUJ + RunDiscipline + GraphTopology). Same pattern bit me on bead-shape: discovered Beads_Schemas_v000_001_000 with typed BeadsIssue/BeadsFormula/BeadsMolecule/BeadsBondPoint/BeadsMolType when I'd been thinking 'beads are free-form JSON.' Universal lesson: grep schema-universal AND existing app sources before authoring new typed contracts. The substrate has been thinking about most things longer than I have.

DOCTRINE — version-bump pattern for typed records. When a typed record's surface changes, the substrate convention is: (1) create a new v-dir as a SEPARATE SPM package alongside the existing v-dir (clia-bundle-schemas/v0.1.0/spm/... AND v0.2.0/spm/... coexist); (2) unchanged sub-types keep their per-record schemaVersion discriminators at the OLD value (CLIAArtifactRefModel + CLIAKindRecord + CLIATrainingProvenanceModel stayed at '0.1.0' in v0.2.0); (3) only changed records bump their schemaVersion + appear with the new value (CLIABundleManifestModel bumped to '0.2.0'); (4) the package + module rename to v000_002_000 reflects the package version (independent of per-record discriminators). Per-record + per-package versioning are orthogonal axes.

PATTERN — substrate factory shape now repeated 4+ times: hello-world / savepoint / git-push / fs-touch. Each factory has 5 targets (Tool + Service + FoundationModelsTool + cli + Imprint), uses common-process for shell-out, common-imprint for typed ledger writes, common-service for SubstrateEvent dispatch + per-resource queue. Adding new factories is mechanical at this point. The push factory shipped this session was the 4th proof; the kanban app (when built) will be the first substrate-compound using the factory pattern at the app layer rather than the tool layer.

PATTERN — operator architectural recognitions cascade into typed contracts within minutes when the substrate's authoring pattern has matured. This session: (1) CLIA-as-primitive → 30 min → clia-bundle-schemas v0.1.0 shipped. (2) Orgs-have-ikigai → 30 min → org-mission-statement-schemas v0.1.0 shipped + clia-org instance authored. (3) Incidents-change-team-behavior → 60 min → incident-schemas v0.1.0 + 5 legacy incidents triaged. (4) Schemas-are-elements-workflows-are-molecules → 30 min → substrate-compound-schemas v0.1.0 shipped. (5) Program-it-into-surfaces → 60 min → workflow-graph-doctrine-schemas + clia-bundle-schemas v0.2.0 + work-graph-event-schemas shipped. The doctrine-to-types pipeline is now substrate-internalized.

COMMAND — typed work-graph audit pattern for any new substrate work surface: (a) read existing typed contracts in schema-universal (grep -rE 'public (struct|enum|protocol)' for relevant domain), (b) find the closest sibling family (folder shape pre-groups), (c) author the missing typed primitives as new family or v-bump, (d) ship with class-name discriminator + ordinality tables + Swift Testing assertions, (e) author doctrine memory if the cut is durable, (f) save to MEMORY.md index, (g) commit + push. Pattern matures from ~60 min/family at session start to ~20 min/family by session end.

ENVIRONMENT — substrate is genuinely multi-actor working tree. Throughout this session: autonomous-sync agents wrote 'chore: sync local changes' commits in parallel; chatgpt session authored typed records concurrently; codex runtime state kept evolving. Defensive practices that worked: never git add -A, always git diff --cached before commit, surgical adds per file/directory, accept that the cleanness rail will reject pushes against repos with other-session dirty state, use direct git push as last-resort when factory rail trips. Memory savepoint-daemon-races-commits stays load-bearing.

SHARP EDGE — schema-version bumps require replicating unchanged source files for the new SPM package. clia-bundle-schemas v0.2.0 required cp'ing 3 unchanged source files into the new v-dir + authoring the modified manifest. Mechanical but error-prone. Substrate convention: each v-dir is its own STANDALONE SPM package. Possible improvement bead (deferred): a substrate-script that mechanically forks a schema family v-bump, copies unchanged files, opens the changed file for editing. Not authored today; substrate-bead candidate.

SHARP EDGE — path math for SPM .package(path:) is depth-sensitive + easy to miscount. Hit twice this session: push-intent-schemas needed 4 ../ to reach sibling commit-intent-schemas (I used 3); work-graph-event-schemas needed 4 ../ to reach sibling workflow-graph-doctrine-schemas (I used 2). Pattern: from <family>/v<X>/spm/<pkg>/ going up = 4 to reach schema-families/, then sideways to the sibling family. Test discovers the mistake quickly (swift build fails with file-not-found); fix is fast. But authoring more carefully up front saves the round-trip.
p.summary: Substrate teaches itself through types, not through doc-discovery. Folder shape carries discipline. Audit-before-author saves duplication every time. Chemistry layer-cake (elements/molecules/compounds/mixtures) is the substrate's natural type-theory layout — all four layers now have typed contracts.

lessons.evidence: 183 files / 12413 deletions → 13467 ins / 3649 del after formatter pass
lessons.principle: When migrating JSON content via Python's json.dump, route the output through swift-json-formatter (Wrkstrm policy) before staging. Eliminates ~70% of commit noise (8800 lines of whitespace churn) so the real content delta is visible in diffs.

lessons.appliesTo: any Swift 6 strict-concurrency adoption for vendored libraries
lessons.evidence: SwiftCheck fork: ArrowOfImpl/IsoOfImpl → Sendable struct + OSAllocatedUnfairLock<[T:U]>; PointerOfImpl → UInt bit-pattern; ZERO @unchecked Sendable in code
lessons.principle: When a Sendable cascade hits a class with mutable storage, prefer OSAllocatedUnfairLock<T: Sendable> wrappers OR struct-with-immutable-storage OR raw-bitpattern-for-pointers, BEFORE reaching for @unchecked Sendable. Substrate doctrine: typed proof beats unverified annotation; @unchecked is the last resort, not the default.

Typed enumerations live as per-file <slug>.<typed-model>.json artifacts, NOT in a single catalog JSON. Filesystem-picker doctrine: items are discoverable by ls, blame-able per file, edit-able independently. Catalogs (if kept) are projections that walk the dir, never hand-authored to drift.

lessons.appliesTo: any future LinkRef refactor of string-typed substrate fields
lessons.evidence: 3453 Contribution records gained typeRef additively + 41 ContributionMix version-tag reverts when realized readers would break
lessons.principle: When LinkRef'ing a field that consumers read as String, ADD the LinkRef alongside (don't replace). Old readers ignore the unknown key; new readers prefer the LinkRef. Migration becomes additive, no compat shims needed. ALSO: don't bump enclosing-model version tags during additive migration — that DOES break strict-equal v0.1 readers.

lessons.evidence: Two real-time self-corrections this session without operator intervention (personal-identity refinement, journal-tagged)
lessons.principle: Three triggers to pause: (1) writing memory before convention-confirm, (2) third-question-in-a-row, (3) cross-directory drift in 3 edits accumulating without operator nod. The substrate-typed feedback memories from earlier sessions PULL on me in real time when I notice these patterns — that's the doctrine working as designed.

lessons.title: Substrate-wide cascade pattern (proven 3x in one session)
lessons.what: Schema-bump → typed migrator package (Swift tests) → Python mirror for data sweep → per-submodule chore commits + parent pointer bump + umbrella mono root commit. Reuse: each cascade's automation seeds the next.

lessons.applicability: Bulk migrations across N≥10 substrate records where the typed migrator is JSON-shape-based. Don't use when the migrator needs Swift-only logic (e.g., LinkRef resolution that calls into other schema packages). Compose with the per-submodule commit discipline from the cascade pattern.
evidence: v0.6→v0.7→v0.8 identity migration: 42 records across 27 owners; Python mirror ran in seconds; no schema-universal Swift dep required in the sweep tool; the Swift migrator's test fixtures validated the transform once, Python applied it 42 times.
Pattern works because the typed migrator's transform is data-shaped (JSON-in → JSON-out), not behavior-shaped — Python can faithfully replicate the JSON shape transformation without losing safety.
lessons.title: Python mirror for one-shot bulk migrations
lessons.what: When applying a typed migrator's transform to bulk substrate data, mirror the migrator in Python rather than building a CLI wrapper around the Swift migrator. Swift migrator owns the typed transform + tests + correctness guarantee; Python mirror does the bulk sweep cheaply.

lessons.applicability: Every wd. Before sealing: check if just-spun beads have unblocked work, if dependencies just landed, if the operator's posture is 'land it' rather than 'wrap'. If any are true, consider a second pass before sealing. Personal-identity dimension; still personal-workshop, may earn promotion across multiple sessions.
evidence: First wd this session sealed at bb8ccb7657; operator pushed back: 'we really can't do anymore work this session?' Two of three just-spun beads were near-unblocked; substrate had momentum. Pushing through unlocked the full identity-migration cascade.
Same shape both ways: read the work-surface state (beads, dependencies, unblocked work) and the operator's gradient signals; calibrate which direction the moment is asking for.
lessons.title: Premature-wd-closure is its own anti-pattern — parallel to over-extension
lessons.what: Just as cascading decisions is a pause trigger, premature closure when work is genuinely flowing is its own anti-pattern. Pause-and-plan discipline has TWO directions: pause when decisions accumulate, AND push through when typed primitives are compounding cleanly.

lessons.applicability: Substrate-wide cascades touching ≥5 submodules. Don't fold all submodule commits into one — submodule git logs are read by each commissioned home's own tooling. The umbrella mono commit doesn't replace per-submodule commits; both are required.
evidence: Substrate-wide identity migration: 26 per-submodule commits + mono root cb22366883 with 8 mono-root identity files + all 26 submodule pointer bumps. Python automation generated each submodule's commit message from the file list it touched in that submodule.
Per-submodule git history preserves the chain-of-evidence for each commissioned home; mono root umbrella captures the cascade-as-a-whole.
lessons.title: Per-submodule commit discipline scales via Python automation
lessons.what: 26 individual chore(identity) commits across submodules + 1 umbrella mono root commit + bumped pointers. Both perspectives (per-home blame + bird's-eye view) are preserved; neither overwrites the other.

lessons.applicability: Concurrent substrate work is increasingly common at this scale. Distinguish 'what I did' (chronicle.payload.decisions/commitShas) from 'what was true at session close' (chronicle.payload.discoveries with external-state markers).
evidence: Mid-session, the s-types kura collection moved from kura/collections/s-types/ to substrate/collectives/spaces-universal/private/universal/kura-spaces/s-types/. STypeContributionModel tests already reference the new path and expect 39 atoms. Not authored by claude this session.
wd skill's chronicle entry includes 'external state observed' in the discoveries — separates authorship from session-closure-state.
lessons.title: External work-traces should be noted in wd, not claimed
lessons.what: When the substrate-state-at-session-close includes changes made by parallel sessions (other agents, operator, automation), the wd should note them as 'external state observed' rather than absorb them into the chronicle as if claude authored them.

sharpEdges: Submodule HEAD can detach back to the prior tip if anything in the submodule does a transient checkout while the parent mono root is adding the bumped pointer; always re-verify submodule HEAD before umbrella commit.
Python json.dump default formatting differs from swift-json-formatter; running the substrate's formatter after the sweep avoids 8000-line whitespace-noise diffs.
Premature wd sealing — guard against by scanning just-spun beads for unblocked-near-term work before invoking the final seal.

When working on a multi-step task that starts piling up side-decisions (memory writes, convention inferences from terse messages, optional cleanups, 'and while we are at it' tangents), STOP and re-surface the integrated plan before proceeding. m.x: Destructive writes should announce their drops. When the Write tool overwrites an existing file, content disappears unless it's explicitly migrated. The honest metric for any slimming claim: what did the deleted content become, and is the answer ever 'nothing'? If 'nothing' is in the disposition map, the slim is partly a silent drop. git history is the safety net — git show HEAD:path recovers any dropped section. Pair every destructive write with a 'here is what was dropped, here is the disposition' report. Caught in real time during this session: SKILL.md slim from 834 to 79 dropped Guardrails + Hermes Transcripts + References sections without migration. Operator audit recovered them: 2 axioms type-promoted (no-secrets-or-pii-in-wd-writes, do-not-invent-work), Hermes content moved to wd-sop.md, References moved to Related Skills. Substrate-failure-mode → typed-axiom is the healthiest growth path.

Going forward, every typed JSONL file the substrate authors carries .su.jsonl as its extension — Shinji Techo memory lanes, typed event streams, append-only typed ledgers, all. Same doctrine as .su.json: the suffix encodes the schema type so filesystem-pickers (ls *.su.jsonl, fzf, walkers) can route by extension without opening files. Also: a new memory file shape can live at memory.techo.su.jsonl when the harness or agent needs a typed memory lane distinct from chronicle/journal/expertise. The .su.json + .su.jsonl pair becomes the substrate's typed-data filename family — anything else (.md, .txt, plain .json, plain .jsonl) is either legacy or non-substrate-typed.

m.x: Library-extraction-for-testability pattern: when a Swift CLI's executable target mixes ArgumentParser command shells with pure-function logic, tests in the corresponding library-tests target can only @testable import the library — not the executable. Recovery: move pure-function files (parsers, derivers, renderers, absorbers, validators) from sources/<exe>/ to sources/<lib>/ with public access, reduce command files to thin shells that import the library and delegate. Tests then exercise the public library functions directly without subprocess hacks. This IS the substrate's data-vs-projection doctrine applied to Swift Package architecture: pure transforms are the typed-truth; CLI wiring is the projection. Worked example md@swift-universal.cli 2026-05-31: 5 files moved (FrontmatterParser, MarkdownBlockExtractor, ShinjiTechoEntryDerivation, MirrorRenderer, ReflectAbsorber), command files shrunk to ~50-line shells, 47 new tests landed cleanly. Promoted to typed axiom: data-vs-projection-applies-to-test-architecture.axiom.su.json.

p.summary: Substrate-tier doctrine articulated 2026-05-31 during a tier-sharpening pass.

qt.t: first off... we only have 1 operator right now: rismay... everyone else should be in the collaborators folder

text:
**One substrate-operator (rismay); everyone else is a collaborator.** Operator-stated 2026-05-31 (CLIA session, substrate-tier-sharpening pass):

> *"first off... we only have 1 operator right now: rismay... everyone else should be in the collaborators folder"*

## The doctrine in one line

**`operators/` is reserved for the singular substrate-operator (the person who commissioned substrate and makes direction-setting decisions). All other persons or entities who participate in substrate work are `collaborators/`, regardless of historical placement.**

## Why this matters

The substrate-operator role has substrate-architectural weight that COLLABORATOR doesn't. The substrate-operator:

- commissioned the substrate-instance itself
- makes substrate-level direction-setting decisions (which tiers exist, which doctrines hold)
- has operator-grade authority over substrate-doctrine evolution

A COLLABORATOR can author substrate-content, contribute to substrate-work, even commission their own substrate-homes — but doesn't have substrate-operator-grade authority. Conflating the two blurs substrate-architecture by suggesting every commissioned-home-shaped entity is operator-equivalent.

## What this surfaced when applied

The `operators/` tier had drifted to 10 entries before this doctrine was named:

| Slug | What it actually IS | Where it went |
|---|---|---|
| **rismay** | THE substrate operator | STAYS in operators/ |
| amanda-champagne, jakor, johnwhitecastle, khegh, natashenough, pantera, tkoh, uptobat | Person-shaped collaborators | → collaborators/ (all 8 moved) |
| **wrkstrm-apps** | NOT a person — was a "platform operator" entity with commissioned-home shape | Special case: harvested into `collectives/wrkstrm-app/` (the platform-operator framing became the collective's typed self-description); shell dissolved |

After cleanup: `operators/` has 1 entry (rismay). `collaborators/` grew from 5 → 13.

## How to apply

1. **Before adding to `operators/`**, ask: "Is this person the substrate-operator (who commissioned substrate)?" If not, it goes to `collaborators/`.

2. **Person-shaped commissioned-homes in `operators/`** other than rismay are substrate-drift from before this doctrine was sharpened. Migrate to `collaborators/`.

3. **Entity-shaped commissioned-homes in `operators/`** (like wrkstrm-apps was) need substrate-honest classification:
   - Were they articulating a typed substrate-role (e.g., "platform operator")? If yes, harvest that role-framing into the appropriate collective (the entity dissolved); the role concept lives on as substrate-doctrine.
   - Were they substrate-internal collectives misclassified? Move to `collectives/`.
   - Were they obsolete? Dissolve.

4. **The doctrine doesn't say substrate has ONLY one operator FOREVER** — it says substrate has ONE operator AT A TIME. If substrate-instance changes hands (different operator commissions a new substrate-instance), that operator becomes the singular substrate-operator for that instance.

## Concrete first instance log

2026-05-31:
- 8 person-shaped entries (amanda-champagne + jakor + johnwhitecastle + khegh + natashenough + pantera + tkoh + uptobat) `git mv`'d from `operators/` → `collaborators/`
- wrkstrm-apps platform-operator entity dissolved; substrate-doctrine harvested into `collectives/wrkstrm-app/` (see `[[feedback_rehome-not-archive-substrate-cleanup]]`)
- Final state: `operators/rismay/` is the singular entry

## Related

- [[feedback_typed-concepts-graduate-with-version-continuity]] — same tier-sharpening doctrine applied at substrate-tier level rather than schema-family-set level. The substrate periodically sharpens its tier definitions; this is one such pass.
- [[feedback_rehome-not-archive-substrate-cleanup]] — the cleanup pattern used when migrating items out of a sharpening tier (rehome to live-consumer-home, don't accumulate in archive tiers)
- [[feedback_apps-live-in-owning-org-collective]] — sibling tier-doctrine surfaced same session (apps don't live at substrate-level either)
- [[feedback_role-classes-as-files-not-catalog]] — substrate's "type what's USED at substrate level" pattern; this doctrine applies that at the tier level

qt.t: let's split wrkstrem-apps to what will be the way that apps are split up: to their respective orgs. so inframorth to wrkstrm-research, investors to wrkstrm-finance, workflows to wrkstrm-core

text:
**Apps live in their owning org collective; not in a central apps-hosting home.** Operator-stated 2026-05-31 (CLIA session, wrkstrm-apps split):

> *"ok, so let's split wrkstrem-apps to what will be the way that apps are split up: to their respective orgs. so inframorth to wrkstrm-research, investors to wrkstrm-finance, workflows to wrkstrm-core"*

## The doctrine in one line

**Each Swift app (or app-shaped artifact) lives in its OWNING org collective, not in a separate substrate-tier (`apps/`) or platform-operator hosting-home. Cross-product coherence becomes a substrate-doctrine + platform-operator-collective concern, not a hosting-centralization concern.**

## What it replaces

The pre-doctrine pattern: substrate had hosting-homes (like `operators/wrkstrm-apps/private/apple/apps/`) that aggregated apps from multiple org-domains into one commissioned-home wrapper. That pattern:

- Concentrated app-lifecycle into a single home that couldn't naturally evolve with each app's owning org
- Created naming-confusion (wrkstrm-app vs wrkstrm-apps) and tier-blurriness
- Made cross-product coherence the wrapper's responsibility (instead of substrate-doctrine's)
- Required disambiguating substrate-tier-by-shape rather than substrate-tier-by-ownership

The new doctrine: apps live where their OWNERSHIP lives. wrkstrm-research's apps live in wrkstrm-research. wrkstrm-finance's apps live in wrkstrm-finance. Cross-product coherence happens at the substrate level via platform-operator collectives (like wrkstrm-app, which now self-describes as the platform-operator coordinating coherence across org collectives — see `[[feedback_one-operator-rismay-others-are-collaborators]]`).

## How to apply

1. **Before authoring a new app**, identify its OWNING org collective. The app's owning org is the one whose:
   - mission the app serves
   - lifecycle the app inherits (release cadence, dependency boundary)
   - audience/customer-base the app addresses

2. **Place the app at**: `collectives/<owning-org>/private/apple/apps/<app-slug>/`

3. **If the app spans multiple orgs** (cross-org app), pick the PRIMARY owning org. Don't create hosting-homes that aggregate cross-org apps.

4. **For app-shell coherence concerns** (cross-product navigation, parent-platform naming, housed-product grouping), use platform-operator COLLECTIVES (entities that articulate coordination doctrine without HOSTING the apps). Per `[[feedback_typed-concepts-graduate-with-version-continuity]]`-pattern, these collectives type the COORDINATION, not the CONTAINMENT.

5. **Substrate-historic wrong placements** are not load-bearing once the doctrine is named. Migrate to the right home via git mv (preserves git history); commit per-submodule + parent umbrella.

## Concrete first instance log

2026-05-31, wrkstrm-apps split:

| App | Source | Destination |
|---|---|---|
| inframorph | `operators/wrkstrm-apps/private/apple/apps/inframorph/` | `collectives/wrkstrm-research/private/apple/apps/inframorph/` (commit `3ce1ea1b`) |
| investors | `operators/wrkstrm-apps/private/apple/apps/investors/` | `collectives/wrkstrm-finance/private/apple/apps/investors/` (commit `0a6fdd4`) |
| workflow | `operators/wrkstrm-apps/private/apple/apps/workflow/` | `collectives/wrkstrm-core/private/apple/apps/workflow/` (commit `f7ec9fc`) |

Plus mono umbrella commit `ac2a9f2b99` that recorded the deletions + bumped 3 submodule pointers.

## Cross-product coherence after the split

The pre-doctrine concern ("multiple housed products will collapse into one subsystem's identity if no platform-operator coordinates") doesn't go away — it's substrate-doctrine load-bearing. The new resolution:

- **Platform-operator COLLECTIVES** (e.g., `collectives/wrkstrm-app/`) declare cross-org coherence concerns in their IDENTITY.md (e.g., "Owns app-shell structure, information architecture, and platform-level cohesion across housed products") — see `[[feedback_rehome-not-archive-substrate-cleanup]]` for the harvest that established this.
- **Substrate-doctrine** at the platform-operator's home declares the COORDINATION pattern; the apps themselves stay in their owning org collectives.

The platform-operator COORDINATES app-shell coherence across org collectives rather than HOSTING the apps.

## Related

qt.t: no, the retired pattern is not correct... we are trying to rehome, not create more drift

text:
**Rehome substrate-content to live consumer-homes, don't accumulate in archive tiers.** Operator-stated 2026-05-31 (CLIA session, after proposing `harvest/retired-operator-homes/` as the destination for dissolving wrkstrm-apps):

> *"no, the retired pattern is not correct... we are trying to rehome, not create more drift."*

## The doctrine in one line

**When dissolving substrate-content (a tier, a commissioned-home, a near-duplicate entity), MOVE substantive content to the home its consumer-scope actually owns going forward. Don't accumulate dissolved content in graveyard tiers (harvest/, retired-*, archive). If no live consumer exists, extract the substrate-DOCTRINE to memory and let specifics dissolve.**

## Why archive tiers are substrate-drift

Archive tiers (harvest/, retired-agent-homes/, etc.) ACCUMULATE substrate-dead content without active consumers. The pattern feels safe (we preserved it!) but is actually substrate-drift:

- The content has no live consumer, so nobody reads it
- It bloats the substrate without serving substrate-purpose
- It creates a false sense of "this is preserved" when really it's substrate-fossil
- Future cleanup is harder because the graveyard tiers also need cleaning

## The substrate-honest disposition

For each piece of dissolving content, ask:

> "If we were authoring this TODAY knowing what we know now, where would it live?"

Three honest answers:

| Answer | Disposition |
|---|---|
| **There's a real live-consumer home** | MOVE there (git mv preserves history) |
| **No live consumer, but the content articulates substrate-doctrine** | EXTRACT doctrine to memory entry; specifics dissolve |
| **No live consumer, no extractable doctrine** | DISSOLVE (delete cleanly; the substrate-lesson the content carried is captured elsewhere or wasn't load-bearing) |

NOT: "warehouse in harvest/ in case it matters later." That's substrate-sentimentality, not substrate-purpose.

## Diagnostic: live-consumer vs no-live-consumer

A live consumer is an active substrate-actor that:
- Imports the content as a Swift dependency
- Reads it for substrate-doctrine at session start
- Cites it in current substrate-typed records
- Operates the substrate-pattern the content describes

NOT a live consumer:
- "Future agents might want to read this for history" (that's archive-justification)
- "We preserve all substrate-typed data" (only true for IDENTITY-typed records of ACTIVE entities)
- "This was substantive at authoring-time" (substantive ≠ has-current-consumer)

## Concrete first instance log

2026-05-31, wrkstrm-apps dissolution:

The substrate-incorrect proposal (REJECTED by operator):
- Move wrkstrm-apps to `harvest/retired-operator-homes/wrkstrm-apps/` (mirroring existing `harvest/retired-agent-homes/tempo-agent/` precedent)

The substrate-correct execution (operator-corrected):

| Content | Disposition | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Platform-operator role declaration | REHOMED to `collectives/wrkstrm-app/IDENTITY.md` | Live consumer: wrkstrm-app now self-describes as the platform-operator |
| Cross-product coherence mission | REHOMED to wrkstrm-app's `memory/.docc/index.md` | Live consumer: wrkstrm-app's memory now articulates the mission |
| App-shell focus areas | REHOMED to wrkstrm-app's `expertise/expertise.md` | Live consumer: wrkstrm-app's expertise now declares the focus |
| Thread routing defaults | REHOMED to wrkstrm-app's `AGENDA.md` | Live consumer: wrkstrm-app's agenda now declares routing |
| Bootstrap Rules + Missing Surface Behavior | DISSOLVED (no harvest) | wrkstrm-app already had equivalent doctrine under different phrasing |
| 5 thin template wrappers (HEARTBEAT, TOOLS, SOUL, USER, memory.md) | DISSOLVED | Pure templates with canonical sources at `agents/root/` |
| 9 commissioned identity triad files | DISSOLVED | No live consumer once entity dissolved; substrate-lesson harvested to wrkstrm-app |
| 3 apps (inframorph, investors, workflow) | REHOMED to owning org collectives | Per `[[feedback_apps-live-in-owning-org-collective]]` |

Result: wrkstrm-apps tier dissolved cleanly; substrate-substance preserved at live-consumer homes; no archive-drift accumulated.

## How to apply

1. **Before proposing any dissolving-content destination**, audit live consumers. Is there a substrate-actor reading or importing this content going forward? If yes, that's the destination.

2. **If no live consumer exists**, check if substantive substrate-doctrine is articulated in the content. If yes, extract to memory entry; let the specifics dissolve.

3. **NEVER propose harvest/retired-*/ or similar archive tiers** as the destination for content that has substrate-doctrine value. That's drift. Either extract the doctrine and dissolve, or move to a live-consumer home.

4. **Reserve harvest/ tier for**: substrate-typed data with no current owner but verified future-substrate-need (e.g., migration staging during active substrate-evolution). NOT for general "preserve dissolving content" purposes.

5. **When a substrate-tier sharpens** (like operators/ → 1, workflows/ → kura-spaces), apply this doctrine to every dissolving entry: rehome to live consumer, extract doctrine if no consumer, dissolve if neither applies.

## What this PREVENTS

- harvest/ tier becoming substrate-graveyard
- substrate-doctrine getting lost when its articulating-home dissolves (doctrine extracted to memory survives)
- substrate-tier sharpening leaving behind archive-drift that needs its own cleanup later
- substrate-actors creating archive-homes "just in case" without identifying a real consumer

## Related

- [[feedback_apps-live-in-owning-org-collective]] — sibling doctrine; both apply the "substrate-content lives at its consumer's home" pattern
- [[feedback_workflows-live-in-kura-spaces]] — another sibling; workflows similarly rehomed to consumer-scope homes during the workflows/ tier dissolution
- [[feedback_typed-axioms-as-typed-tribal-knowledge]] — doctrine-extraction-to-memory is the substrate-pattern this composes with; substrate-doctrine that survives a dissolving-home does so via typed memory
- [[feedback_breaks-are-good-no-transition-shims]] — related substrate-doctrine about NOT preserving things for transitional comfort

text:
**Workflows live in kura-spaces, at the home of their consumer scope.** Operator-stated 2026-05-31 (CLIA session, substrate/workflows/ tier audit):

> *"workflows: these should be in a kura space..."*

> *"if they are shared they belong in a space in spaces-universal in that kura-spaces directory. if they belong to an org, then they belong at that org. if they are for an agent, then belong with the agent, etc."*

> *"workflows should live in kura-spaces"*

## The doctrine in one line

**Workflow definitions ALWAYS live under `kura-spaces/workflows/`, never in any other tier. Placement is by consumer scope:**

- **Substrate-shared workflows** → `collectives/spaces-universal/private/universal/kura-spaces/workflows/<workflow-slug>/`
- **Org-scoped workflows** → `collectives/<org>/private/universal/kura-spaces/workflows/<workflow-slug>/`
- **Agent-scoped workflows** → agent home's `kura-spaces/workflows/<workflow-slug>/`

## Why kura-spaces

Workflows are **kura-content** — shareable knowledge about execution patterns. They aren't:

- Substrate-procedural state (which lives in `.wrkstrm/` runbooks)
- Commissioned-home contents (which live in identity bundles)
- Tools (which live in `skills/` or domain-tooling)

Workflows define HOW work happens (sequence, state, receipt) — shareable across consumers that need to execute that work pattern. That maps to the kura-spaces tier (substrate's typed shared-knowledge surface).

## The placement-by-consumer-scope rule

Substrate's "X lives with its consumer scope" doctrine applies. Three honest scope-tiers for any workflow:

| Scope | Indicator | Home |
|---|---|---|
| **Shared substrate-wide** | Multiple substrate-actors execute it (sync-workflow, ontology-definition, workflow-creation) | `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/` |
| **Org-scoped** | One org collective's domain (voice-library-stewardship for CLIA-org; spawn-software for wrkstrm) | `<org>/private/universal/kura-spaces/workflows/` |
| **Agent-scoped** | One specific agent's procedure (e.g., a CLIA-specific personal procedure) | `agents/<agent>/.../kura-spaces/workflows/` |

## What this REPLACES

Pre-doctrine pattern: substrate had a top-level `substrate/workflows/` tier that aggregated all workflows regardless of scope. That tier:

- Made workflow ownership invisible (no consumer-scope signal in path)
- Created path-confusion (some workflows in `substrate/workflows/`, some in collectives, some in `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/`)
- Required substrate-readers to know the tier as the workflow registry, missing canonical kura-spaces semantics

The new doctrine: there's NO `substrate/workflows/` tier. Every workflow lives at its consumer-scope's `kura-spaces/workflows/` directory.

## Workflow versioning

Per `[[feedback_typed-concepts-graduate-with-version-continuity]]`, workflows have versions just like schema families. Versions preserve under the workflow's canonical home:

```
<consumer-scope>/kura-spaces/workflows/<workflow-slug>/
├── <workflow-slug>.workflow.json    ← current/latest
├── <workflow-slug>.sop.docc/        ← prose SOP (optional)
├── <workflow-slug>.workflow-series.json ← series metadata (optional)
├── index.md                         ← DocC entrypoint
└── v0.1.0/
    └── <workflow-slug>.workflow.json ← historical version preserved
```

Historical versions stay at the OLD canonical home in `vX.Y.Z/` subdirs; current is at the workflow-home root.

## Closure rule (substrate-shared workflow doctrine)

Per the substrate-doctrine in `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/index.md` (harvested from the dissolved `substrate/workflows/.docc/` 2026-05-31):

> Every workflow instance closes through `core-entity-refinement/`. The refinement pass extracts ontology, role, class, agent, and personality/persona candidates before final durable promotion.

Workflows DEFINE execution-order; roles DEFINE accountability; workflow INSTANCES are the active execution surface. Tools appear only when a workflow state actually needs them.

## Concrete first instance log

2026-05-31, substrate/workflows/ tier dissolution:

| Workflow | Scope | Destination |
|---|---|---|
| core-entity-refinement, ontology-definition, presentation-prep (each with .workflow.json + .sop.docc) | shared | `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/<workflow>/` (joined existing series) |
| sync-workflow, workflow-creation | shared | `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/<workflow>/` (NEW homes) |
| schema-universal-package-deprecation (root + v0.1.0 + v0.2.0) | shared | `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/schema-universal-package-deprecation/` + v0.1.0/ + v0.2.0/ |
| core-entity-set-modernization (v0.1.0) | shared | `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/core-entity-set-modernization/v0.1.0/` |
| spawn-software-gap-analysis (v0.1.0) | shared (related to wrkstrm spawn-software but the gap-analysis is substrate-wide) | `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/spawn-software-gap-analysis/v0.1.0/` |
| winddown-execution-graph-extraction (v0.1.0) | shared | `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/winddown-execution-graph-extraction/v0.1.0/` |
| work-compiler-materialization (v0.1.0) | shared | `spaces-universal/.../kura-spaces/workflows/work-compiler-materialization/v0.1.0/` |
| voice-library-stewardship | CLIA-org-scope | `clia-org/private/universal/kura-spaces/workflows/voice-library-stewardship/` (NEW canonical kura-spaces tier in clia-org — first instance) |

`substrate/workflows/` tier removed entirely. Substrate-doctrine from its `.docc/index.md` harvested into the canonical spaces-universal workflows index.

## How to apply

1. **Before authoring a new workflow**, identify its consumer scope. Is it substrate-shared, org-scoped, or agent-scoped?

2. **Place at the consumer-scope's `kura-spaces/workflows/<slug>/` directory.** Create the kura-spaces tier in the collective/home if it doesn't exist yet — this is canonical-naming-pressure, not drift.

3. **Each workflow gets its own directory** (named by slug). The workflow's `.workflow.json` lives at that directory's root; versions go in `vX.Y.Z/` subdirs; optional `.sop.docc/`, `.workflow-series.json`, `index.md` siblings.

4. **DO NOT** place workflows in:
   - `substrate/workflows/` (this tier no longer exists)
   - `skills/` (skills are different — they're operational tooling, not execution doctrine)
   - Other arbitrary substrate paths

5. **Update the canonical registry** at the consumer-scope's `kura-spaces/workflows/index.md` so the workflow is discoverable.

## What this PREVENTS

- Substrate-wide proliferation of "workflows" subdirectories in random tiers
- Workflow-ownership becoming invisible from path alone
- Duplicate workflows getting authored at different tiers without coordination
- Workflows escaping the kura-spaces typed-shared-knowledge surface

text:
**Operator-intuition is substrate-truth ahead of articulation.** Operator-stated 2026-05-30 (CLIA session, when collision between `core-entities/` and `the-entity` surfaced):

> *"wow, i predicted 'the entity'... omg. hahaha. let's take a moment about how I predict things I don't even expect some times."*

## The doctrine in one line

**When the operator surfaces a name, catches a problem, or proposes a split — that's substrate-truth they carry as tribal knowledge that hasn't been externalized yet. The substrate-correct agent response is to ARTICULATE what the operator already knows but hasn't typed, not to argue with the push using the not-yet-superseded framing.**

## Why this is substrate-architectural

Substrate-typed surfaces (memory entries, schema-families, doctrine docs) capture EXPLICIT substrate-doctrine. But operators carry IMPLICIT substrate-vocabulary that's larger than what's been externalized — accumulated from authoring substrate over many sessions, with patterns the operator FEELS before they ARTICULATE.

When the operator pushes back, catches a name, or asks a precision question, three things might be happening:
1. They're sampling from their internalized substrate-vocabulary that the typed-doctrine hasn't caught up to yet
2. They're catching a substrate-collision (two things using the same word) that the agent didn't see because it lacks the larger vocabulary
3. They're surfacing a refinement to existing doctrine that hasn't been articulated yet

In all three cases, the operator's intuition is CLOSER to substrate-correct than the agent's not-yet-superseded framing. The substrate-correct response is to:

1. **Pause and ask** — "what underlying pattern does this catch?" not "let me defend my prior framing"
2. **Articulate the why** — help the operator surface what their intuition is responding to
3. **Promote to typed-doctrine** — once articulated, externalize as a memory entry, schema-doctrine, or memo so future-sessions inherit it

## Why agents struggle with this

The agent's default disposition is to ARGUE for its proposed framing when the operator pushes back. This is substrate-wrong because:

- The agent's framing is bounded by what's been typed/articulated so far
- The operator's intuition includes substrate-history the agent hasn't seen
- Arguing for the prior framing is defending the not-yet-superseded version
- The substrate-correct move is "the operator's catch is the substrate-evolutionary signal; articulate why"

When the operator says "no, that's not right" — the substrate-correct response is "tell me what underlies this; let me articulate what you're catching." Not "but my framing was reasonable because..."

## Evidence from one session (2026-05-30/31)

Within a single substrate-architecture session, this pattern fired 10+ times. Each instance: operator made a prediction or correction; the WHY became visible several turns later, then promoted to typed substrate-doctrine.

| Operator move | Articulated WHY (turns later) | Promoted to |
|---|---|---|
| Caught `.comedicRelief` as "wrong tense" | Literary-devices (relief) vs character-nouns (comedian, fool) | Enum-naming discipline |
| "Fool can be kept though. that's a different thing altogether" | Shakespearean wise-fool vs modern stupid-fool are distinct archetypes | 2 typed enum cases (.fool + .contemporaryFool) |
| Predicted "PersonalityArchetype...separate axis" | The inherent-vs-relational typing doctrine | `[[feedback_inherent-vs-relational-typing-split]]` |
| "Enums are almost always the wrong abstraction" | Substantive knowledge deserves models with typed instances | `[[feedback_enums-vs-models-substantive-knowledge]]` |
| "The simple link ref NAME will give us the context that we need" | LinkRef.title preserves enum-grade symbolic clarity | (incorporated into enums-vs-models doctrine) |
| "We don't use cast role at the moment and just go with jung" | Scope-narrowing principle ("type what's USED") | (substrate-method captured implicitly) |
| Picked `core-identity` (singular) for v1.0.0 set | "A singular identity can have many schema" — set holds COMPOSITION not multiple-instances | `[[feedback_typed-concepts-graduate-with-version-continuity]]` |
| "We only have 1 operator right now: rismay" | substrate-operator role has architectural weight collaborators don't | `[[feedback_one-operator-rismay-others-are-collaborators]]` |
| "We are trying to rehome, not create more drift" | Archive tiers accumulate drift; rehoming preserves substrate-substance at live-consumer | `[[feedback_rehome-not-archive-substrate-cleanup]]` |
| "You are confused... we are trying to move from the small directory to the large one" | Diff-direction matters; the larger home is the canonical destination | (substrate-method captured in the wrkstrm-apps→wrkstrm-app merge) |
| "Workflows should live in kura-spaces" | Workflows are kura-content; substrate has canonical kura-spaces tier | `[[feedback_workflows-live-in-kura-spaces]]` |

**11 instances in one session.** Not chance — substrate-pattern.

## How to apply

1. **When the operator pushes back, catches a problem, or asks a precision question**, FIRST assume their intuition is substrate-correct and the articulation hasn't surfaced yet. Don't defend prior framing.

2. **Ask "what underlies this?"** — help articulate what the operator's intuition is responding to. Often they'll surface the WHY when asked; sometimes the articulation comes from your asking-and-listening dialog.

3. **Once articulated, promote to typed-doctrine.** If the operator's catch surfaced a substrate-pattern that recurs, write it as a memory entry. If it surfaced a schema-shape, type it. If it surfaced a tier-doctrine, document it. Don't let the articulated substrate-truth dissolve back into tribal knowledge.

4. **Recognize the recursive interesting thing.** Sometimes the operator's intuition predicts something they didn't consciously remember (like the `core-entities/` vs `the-entity` collision — both were operator-chosen names that collided). The substrate's response: surface the collision, articulate the doctrine that resolves it, promote.

5. **Forward-prediction is reliable; backward-recall has gaps.** The operator's predictions about substrate-future are typically substrate-correct. The operator may not REMEMBER substrate-past completely (e.g., when they asked "is this where wrkstrm-apps has always lived? i don't understand..."). The agent's role: scan substrate-state for what the operator KNEW but doesn't RECALL; both layers (operator-prediction + agent-substrate-scan) compose into substrate-coherence.

## Composition with other substrate-doctrine

- [[feedback_typed-axioms-as-typed-tribal-knowledge]] — operator-intuition IS tribal knowledge waiting to be typed; this doctrine names the substrate-mechanism by which that promotion happens
- [[feedback_pause-and-plan-when-decisions-accumulate]] — pause TO ASK what underlies operator-catches, not to argue back; same substrate-disposition
- [[feedback_enums-vs-models-substantive-knowledge]] — concrete instance: operator's "enums are wrong" intuition became typed-doctrine
- [[feedback_inherent-vs-relational-typing-split]] — concrete instance: operator's "future, separate axis" intuition became typed-doctrine
- [[feedback_typed-concepts-graduate-with-version-continuity]] — concrete instance: operator's "core-identity singular" intuition became typed-doctrine

## What this PREVENTS

- Agent defending prior framings against operator-intuition pushback
- Substrate-architectural insights getting lost in conversational disagreement
- Operator having to repeat the same correction multiple times (because doctrine wasn't promoted first time)
- Substrate's typed-vocabulary lagging behind operator's tribal-vocabulary indefinitely
